front: Group: sacked Rome under their king Alaric I in 410 CE
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Group: ruled a kingdom in Iberia (Spain) after the fall of Rome, contrasted with their eastern-branch cousins who ruled Italy under Theodoric the Great
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Event: under the leaders Alavivus and Fritigern, these people crossed the Danube into the Roman Empire in 376 CE seeking refuge from the advancing Huns
work: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople

front: Event: this Roman official mistreated the Visigoths, forcing them to sell their children into slavery for dog meat, before being routed by them at Marcianopolis
work: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople

front: War: under Fritigern, the Visigoths defeated and killed Emperor Valens at this 378 CE battle
work: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople

front: Work: this historian wrote the Getica, the chief ancient history of the Goths
work: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople

front: Figure: this Visigothic noble dynasty, whose name meant "bold," rivaled the Ostrogothic Amali dynasty for supremacy
work: Origins: Crossing the Danube & the Battle of Adrianople

front: Ruler: this Visigothic king, who led the 410 sack of Rome, died shortly afterward and was buried beneath the diverted Busento River
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Figure: this Roman general's execution precipitated the Visigothic sack of Rome; he had earlier fought alongside them at the Battle of the Frigidus before crushing Alaric at the Battle of Pollentia
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Figure: this sister of Emperor Honorius was abducted by the Visigoths during the sack of Rome
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Work: the Visigothic sack of Rome inspired this book by St. Augustine, defending Christianity against critics who blamed it for Rome's fall
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Event: during the sack of Rome, this emperor (safe in Ravenna) was reportedly more relieved that his pet chicken had survived than concerned about the city
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Figure: this saint lamented in a letter written during the sack of Rome, "if Rome can perish, what can be safe?"
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Figure: Alaric installed this puppet usurper before deposing him when negotiations with Rome collapsed
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Figure: this historian defended the sack of Rome as having prevented the usurper Radagaisus from seizing power in its aftermath
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Event: Alaric reportedly demanded a bribe of 5,000 pounds of gold and 3,000 pounds of pepper to call off his attack on Rome
work: The Sack of Rome (410 CE)

front: Period: the Visigoths established their first post-Roman kingdom with a capital at this southern Gaulish city
work: The Kingdom of Toulouse & the Battle of Chalons

front: War: a Visigothic king allied with the Roman general Flavius Aetius to defeat Attila the Hun at this battle, dying in the fighting
work: The Kingdom of Toulouse & the Battle of Chalons

front: War: the Frankish king Clovis I defeated and killed the Visigothic king Alaric II at this 507 battle
work: The Battle of Vouille & Retreat to Spain

front: Event: after losing their Gallic territories to the Franks, the Visigoths retreated permanently to Spain and established a new capital at this city
work: The Battle of Vouille & Retreat to Spain

front: Ruler: this Visigothic king converted from Arianism to Catholicism at the urging of Bishop Leander of Seville, then convened the Third Council of Toledo (589), which codified the filioque clause
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Figure: this bishop, with his brother Leander, helped eliminate Arianism from the Visigothic kingdom and compiled the encyclopedic Etymologiae
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Law: this Visigothic king codified their laws in the Liber Judiciorum ("Book of the Judges"), ending separate legal codes for Goths and Hispano-Romans
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Law: this earlier Visigothic law code compiled Roman law for the non-ruling Hispano-Roman majority, alongside the Code of Euric
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Ruler: this Visigothic king banished (and likely had murdered) his son Hermenegild for converting to Catholicism and revolting, but issued the Codex Revisus permitting intermarriage between Goths and Hispano-Romans
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Event: this hoard of 26 gold votive crowns and crosses, including one from King Recceswinth set with Sri Lankan sapphires, was offered by Visigothic kings to the Church
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Event: roughly thirty Visigothic church councils held at Toledo gave rise to this distinct liturgical rite
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Institution: Visigothic legal assemblies are considered an ancestor of this later Spanish parliamentary institution
work: Religion & Law: Arianism, the Councils of Toledo & Legal Codes

front: Ruler: this last Visigothic king was defeated and killed by the Umayyad general Tariq ibn Ziyad at the Battle of Guadalete (711)
work: Fall to the Umayyads: the Battle of Guadalete (711)

front: Event: the Visigoths lost this capital city to the Umayyads shortly after their defeat at the Battle of Guadalete
work: Fall to the Umayyads: the Battle of Guadalete (711)

front: Work: this anonymous text is the chief primary source for the Battle of Guadalete
work: Fall to the Umayyads: the Battle of Guadalete (711)

front: Event: Christians who continued living under Muslim rule in conquered Visigothic Spain (al-Andalus) became known by this term
work: Fall to the Umayyads: the Battle of Guadalete (711)

front: Figure: this Visigothic nobleman founded the Kingdom of Asturias and won the Battle of Covadonga (722), traditionally marking the start of the Reconquista
work: Legacy: The Reconquista

front: War: the Reconquista later reached a turning point at this 1212 battle
back: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
work: Legacy: The Reconquista
